coredata.dc:description: Within this research, the possibility of using hexagonal boron nitride quantum dots (HBNQDs) and edge-functionalized h-BNQDs (EFHBNQDs) as hole transport materials (HTMs) was explored in perovskite solar cells (PVSCs). The performance, hole mobility (HM), hole reorganization energy (λ h), exciton binding energy (E b), light harvesting efficiency (LHE) and solubility of these HTMs were investigated to determine their suitability. Based on the results, in contrast with Spiro-OMeTAD that is commonly used as an HTM in PVSCs, these HTMs had a greater HM. Also, their fill factor and open circuit voltage were computed to be suitable, similar to those of MAPbI3-based PVSCs. Thanks to their high performance, small λ h and high solubility, H-BNQD-SCH3 were considered to be the most ideal HTMs.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Within this research, the possibility of using hexagonal boron nitride quantum dots (HBNQDs) and edge-functionalized h-BNQDs (EFHBNQDs) as hole transport materials (HTMs) was explored in perovskite solar cells (PVSCs). The performance, hole mobility (HM), hole reorganization energy (λ h), exciton binding energy (E b), light harvesting efficiency (LHE) and solubility of these HTMs were investigated to determine their suitability. Based on the results, in contrast with Spiro-OMeTAD that is commonly used as an HTM in PVSCs, these HTMs had a greater HM. Also, their fill factor and open circuit voltage were computed to be suitable, similar to those of MAPbI3-based PVSCs. Thanks to their high performance, small λ h and high solubility, H-BNQD-SCH3 were considered to be the most ideal HTMs.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: No data was used for the research described in the article.

ce:section.ce:para: Renewable energy generation has gained significant attention in recent years as a viable alternative to fossil fuels for electricity generation. The use of renewable energy sources, such as solar, wind, hydrogen, hydro power, can help reduce greenhouse gas emissions and combat climate change [1–5]. However, the intermittent nature of some renewable energy sources, such as sun and wind, presents a challenge for a reliable power supply. Energy storages, such as batteries, play a crucial role in overcoming this intermittency, allowing generated energy to be stored for later use. Batteries can store energy for a short period of time, such as in electric vehicles, or for a long period of time, such as in grid-scale applications [6–10]. The development of efficient and cost-effective energy storage technologies is essential for the widespread adoption of renewable energy generation and the transition to a sustainable energy future. Thanks to their unique properties such as easy integration (through roll-to-roll fabrication methods), cost-effectiveness and high efficiency, hole transport materials (HTMs) are regarded as important by scientists and different industries in optimizing perovskite solar cells (PVSCs) [11–13]. With the advent of PVSCs, the photovoltaic technology has underwent a dramatic transformation since the efficiency of photovoltaics has increased from 3.7 % to 25.8 % within a very short period of time, which makes PVSCs promising alternatives to conventional silicon SCs [14]. The light-absorbing efficiency of PVSCs has positioned them between the HTMs and the electron transport materials (ETMs). HTMs are primarily responsible for extracting holes while preventing charge recombination at the interfaces between the HTMs and the perovskite layers [15]. HTMs must have crucial properties, such as high mobility, high conductance, high hole transport capacity, and the must be highly soluble, so that they can be considered suitable for application in PVSCs [16,17]. The choice of HTMs dramatically affects the effectiveness of PSC-based devices as HTMs have important functions, namely creating specialized contacts for carriers and changing the energy barrier heights which exist between the active layers and the electrodes. Also, it is worth mentioning that HTMs can provide protective barriers, which protect the active layers from chemical or physical interactions that might occur between the electrodes and the active layers. Furthermore, they are capable of improving charge collection and transmission processes, thus improving the overall efficiency of the devices. Ultimately, HTMs are capable of improving the stability of both electrodes and active layers, which can directly impact upon the overall stability of the devices [14,17].

One of the commonly used compounds as an HTM in advancing PVSCs is 2,2′,7,7′-tetrakis-(N,N-di-p-methoxyphenylamine), which is also known as Spiro-OMeTAD (hereafter STAD). STAD has high conductance after doping, and its amorphous structure is stable. Also, it is capable of generating thin films [18–26]. Nonetheless, researchers have been urged to find alternative materials due to its high synthesis cost [27]. For example, researchers have developed several inexpensive and simple materials such as azomethine [28], silolothiophene [29], azine [30], furan [31], carbazole [32], phenylamines [33] and fluorene [34–39].

Owing to their unique properties, which are conducive to developing next-generation technology, two-dimensional quantum dots (2D QDs) have attracted the attention of various research groups from various fields. One of the promising materials with many photovoltaic applications, such as photocatalysis [40–44], bioimaging [45], optical sensing [46–50] and energy conversion [51–55], is the hexagonal boron nitride monolayer (hereafter HBNML). Also, the basal plane of the HBNML has strong ionic properties, which are conducive to delocalizing electrons because the difference between the electronegativity of B and that of N is big [56]. Also, HBNMLs are ultra-flat with an impurity free space, which makes them suitable for van der Waals heterostructures [57]. HBNML quantum dots (HBNQDs) can be fabricated by reducing the dimensions along the lateral directions of the HBNML, further constraining the wave-functions. Although many studies have been done on nano-ribbons [30] and HBN nanotubes [29], researchers have recently investigated QDs. Similar to graphene QDs, the shape, edge type and size of HBNQDs determine their photo-physical properties [58]. For example, a combination of Landauer transport theory and Green's non-equilibrium function approach was used to explore the thermo-electric impact of the HBNQDs [59]. The thermopower of HBNQDs is greater compared to that of graphene QDs (GrQDs) since the phonon conductance is suppressed by the interfaces in HNBQDs. They may have used various methods, such as hydrothermal synthesis, solvothermal synthesis, and microwave-assisted synthesis, among others, to synthesize boron nitride quantum dots s. These methods are relatively cost-effective and have been shown to produce high-quality boron nitride quantum dots with good stability. Additionally, these methods are less toxic compared to other methods, which can reduce the environmental impact of the synthesis process and make the materials more viable for applications in the field of solar cells. Thanks to their unique properties and potential applications as luminous nano-materials, researchers have recently investigated carbon QDs (CQDs) [60–62], which were discovered in 2004 for the first time by Xu et al. Later, researchers conducted further studies into synthesizing, characterizing and using them [63,64]. In the literature, researchers have investigated GQDs as variants of CQDs. They mainly consist of C atoms and their dimension is less than 10 nm, which is very small [65]. Nevertheless, their bandgap can be adjusted by altering their properties through functionalization. CQDs are the C atoms at the periphery of GQDs, completely saturated through H atoms [66]. Thanks to their potential application in photosynthesis, researchers have performed numerous studies on CQDs. Recently, many studies have been conducted on the application of CQDs, especially GQDs, as high-performance HTMs in PVSCs because of their unique properties, including cost-effectiveness, non-toxicity, stability and their environmentally-friendly nature [67–69]. Testsuka et al. developed a method to produce N-functionalized GQDs (NGQDs) whose optical properties can be adjusted. The performance of hybrid phototransistors and PVSCs was dramatically enhanced after the incorporation of NGQD layers into opto-electronic devices [67]. The current work focuses on utilizing DFT to explore the effect of edge functionalization on the efficacy of HBNQDs used as HTMs in PVSCs.

The current work highlights the importance of using doped hole transport materials (HTMs) in perovskite solar cells (PSCs) to achieve high efficiency. It emphasizes that using a non-dopant material may not result in a successful photovoltaic device and that the choice of the dopant material is critical in achieving high performance in devices. The current work also highlights the difficulties in obtaining a high-quality doping process due to the low solubility of the dopant material and the presence of impurities. The current work focuses on investigating the possibility of using HBNQDs edge-functionalized EFHBNQDs as HTMs in PVSCs. One of the effective factors in improving the performance of PVSCs is the choice of suitable HTMs. A number of factors, such as the performance, hole mobility (HM), hole reorganization energy (λ h), exciton binding energy (E b), light harvesting efficiency (LHE) and the solubility of HTMs, were evaluated to check their suitability.

The HBNQDs and three EFHBNQDs were optimized using the Berny optimization algorithm [70]. The displacement thresholds for RMS, maximum displacement, RMS force and maximum force, respectively, were set to 1.2 × 10−3, 1.8 × 10−3 Bohr, 3.0 × 10−4 and 4.5 × 10−4 Hartree/Bohr. The conductor-like PCM was adopted to investigate the impact of the dichloromethane solvent (DS) [71]. The self-consistence field (SCF) convergence criterion was set to 10−6. For the optimizations, the maximum displacement, that is, the maximum structural change of one coordinate and the average (RMS) change over all structural parameter thresholds were set to 1.8 × 10−4 and 1.2 × 10−4, respectively. The maximum remaining force on an atom in the system and the average root mean square (RMS) force on all atoms thresholds were set to be about 4.5 × 10−4 and 3.0 × 10−4, respectively. There were no imaginary frequencies in the frequency calculations undertaken on the pure and EFHBNQDs. The time-dependent DFT computations were performed to obtain the UV/Vis spectra [72]. The LHE and E b of HBNQDs were computed as follows [73]:

where E opt, E g and f abs max represent the optical bandgap, the bandgap between the HOMO and LUMO and the oscillator strength for the maximum absorption peak respectively. The Gaussian software was employed to optimize the geometries using the B3LYP-D3 [74] and the basis set 6-31G**. Additionally, The time-dependent DFT and NBO computations were performed using the B3LYP-D3 together with def2svp [75]. The electronic structures of QDs can be simulated with high accuracy using B3LYP-D3/def2svp [69].

ce:section.ce:section-title: The solubility and geometry of HBNQDs
ce:section.ce:para: Fig. 1 shows the optimized geometry of the pure and EFHBNQDs and BNQD-Y, (X = CH3, OCH3, and SCH3) in the gaseous phase. One of the important factors in developing organic electronic devices (OEDs) is the solubility of HTMs, which determines the dissolving capacity of substances into solvents and forming homogenous solutions. Solubility affects the thin-film making ability of materials, which affects the performance of electronic devices [76]. The solvation energy of HTMs was obtained using the formula ΔE solvation = E solution − E gas. Here, the molecular energy in the solvent and gas phases was designated by E solution and E gas respectively [77]. Table 1 lists the ΔE solvation of the HBNQDs and EFHBNQDs in the DS. Based on the results, HBNQD-SCH3, HBNQD-OCH3 and HBNQD-CH3 had the highest ΔE solvation of − 22.14 kcal mol−1, −18.66 and − 15.37 respectively. The findings indicated that HBNQD-SCH3, HBNQD-OCH3 and HBNQD-CH3 had the highest solubility amongst the HTMs. This is due to the fact that the H-bonding capability of the substituents (–SCH3 and –OCH3) with DS was strong.

The dipole moment of HBNQDs and EFHBNQDs in the DS and gaseous phases is reported in Table 1. Electron-carrier mobility (ECM) is inversely related to the dipole moment. The ECM becomes lower when the dipole moment becomes greater, which also increases the hole-carrier mobility and the performance of OEDs. HBNQDs are non-polar compounds because their structure is symmetric, leading to the lack of dipole moment. Nonetheless, their symmetry can be broken and their polarity and dipole moment can be enhanced by introducing edge functionalization. Amongst the EFHBNQDs, the polarity of HBNQD-OCH3, and HBNQD-SCH3 was the highest, and their DM was 3.89 and 4.96 D in gaseous phase, respectively. Moreover, the polarity of EFHBNQDs could be increased by dissolving them in the DS.

There is relationship between the hole transportation via HTMs and the band gaps between their HOMO and LUMO levels, which have a significant impact upon the performance of PVSCs. The schematic representations of HOMO and LUMO of the HBNQDs and EFHBNQDs in the DS are shown in Fig. 2 . In addition, the HOMO and LUMO energies of the HBNQDs and EFBNQDs are provided in Table 2 . The HOMO level of molecules must be greater than the valence band of the perovskite so that they can be considered suitable for application in PVSCs. Many studies have been done on MAPbI3 (methylammonium lead iodide), which is one of the widely used perovskite materials because of its potential application in SCs. The power conversion efficiency (PCE), which measures the light conversion ability of SCs into electricity, of MAPbI3 -based PVSCs is high, as shown in the literature [78–80]. The MAPbI3 had a valence band of − 5.43 eV [81]. The HOMO energy of HBNQDs and EFHBNQDs were greater compared to the HOMO energy of MAPbI3, as shown in Table 3 and Fig. 1S, which indicated that they are suitable HTMs for MAPbI3-based SCs.

The hole transport capacity of HTMs to the Au cathode is also a requirement when considering their application in PVSCs. the HOMO level of HTMs must be situated below the fermi level (FL) of the Au cathode, i.e. − 5.1 eV, to meet this requirement [82–84]. As shown in Table 2, the HOMO level of HBNQD-OCH3 and HBNQD-SCH3 was below the FL of the Au cathode. It should be noted that the HOMO energy of HBNQD-OCH3 and HBNQD-SCH3 was more than that of the STAD, which is − 5.20 eV [85]. Hence, we can predict that HBNQD-OCH3 and HBNQD-SCH3 are better than STAD for application as HTMs. The LUMO level of suitable HTMs must be situated above the conduction band (CB) of MAPbI3 perovskite (−3.93 eV) in addition to a properly positioned HOMO [81]. The reason for this is the prevention of the flowing of photo-generated electrons from the MAPbI3 layer back toward the Au electrode. The LUMO level of HBNQDs and EFHBNQDs were located above the CB of MAPbI3.

The time-dependent DFT was employed to obtain the UV/Vis spectra for the HBNQDs and EFHBNQDs. As shown in Fig. 3 , the UV/Vis spectra with a half width at half height (HWHH) was 0.33 eV. The UV/Vis spectra of HBNQDs and EFHBNQDs exhibited dramatic red shift. For the HBNQDs, a peak was observed in the range of 250 nm, whereas for both HBNQD-OCH3 and HBNQDs-SCH3, wide and sharp peaks were observed in the range of 290 (330) nm and 650–950 (420–900) nm, respectively. Moreover, the HBNQDs-SCH3 exhibited a peak in the range of 335 nm, and this compound exhibited a heightened redshift in comparison with other HBNQDs. The short sharp peak can be ascribed to the excitation of electrons from S0 → S1, corresponding to the excitations of electrons from HOMO to the LUMO. In addition, based on the LHE of the HBNQDs and EFHBNQDs in Table 4 , it can be deduced that their light absorption capability is high. As can be seen, HBNQDs and HBNQD-SCH3 had the highest LHE of 0.99, whereas HBNQD-CH2 had the LHE of 0.97.

The energy needed for S0 → S1 shows the optical band-gap (E opt). According to Eq. (2), the exciton binding energy (E b), which shows the coulombic attraction strength between the holes and the excited electrons, is defined as the difference between E g and E opt. A large E b suggests that the electron–hole interactions are strong, leading to a reduction in the charge separation efficiency, thus decreasing the overall performance of devices, especially for SCs, where charge separation efficiency is important for greater power conversion efficacy. However, a negligible E b indicates that the electron–hole interactions are weak, which can facilitate the charge separation efficiency and transfer, which in turn makes the material more suitable for opto-electronic applications. The E b of the EFHBNQDs ranged from 1.35 to 0.19 eV (Table 4), which indicated that the electron–hole interactions were weak and the charge transfer was high in these materials.

After the loss of electrons, systems undergo structural relaxation, leading to the energy, which is defined as the hole reorganization energy (λ h) and it can be computed as follows [86]:

Moreover, E 0 + and E 0 represent the neutral energy of optimized cationic and neutral structures. In organic semiconductors, a high charge carrier mobility is considered important, which can be achieved by minimizing λ h [86]. A comparison of the λ h for HBNQDs and EFHBNQDs is provided in Table 5 . As shown in Table 5, apart from the HBNQD-SCH2, the λ h of HBNQDs and EFHBNQDs was less than the λ h of STAD (163.31 meV) [86], which suggests their superior mobility compared to STAD.

Table 5 lists the ν h, k h,
i and ri and the HM of HBNQDs and EFHBNQDs. As can be seen, HBNQDs and EFHBNQDs had greater HM than STAD (2 × 10−4 to 6 × 10−3 cm2 V−1 s−1) [87]. Amongst the QDs, HBNQD-SCH3 had the greatest HM, which was 0.79 cm2 V−1 s−1. Several recent experimental and theoretical studies have explored the use of different HTMs in PSCs, which provides valuable insights into the factors that influence their performance [88–90]. For instance, a recent experimental study by Liu et al. demonstrated that the use of a bilayer HTM, consisting of a small molecule and a polymer, resulted in improved device performance compared to those using a single HTM. Furthermore, a theoretical study by Kim et al. [88] found that the use of a triiodide dopant in the HTM led to improved device performance through improved electronic coupling between the HTM and the perovskite material. In addition, several theoretical studies have utilized DFT and TDDFT calculations to evaluate the properties of various HTMs. For example, a recent study by Wang et al. used DFT calculations to investigate the properties of a new HTM based on tri(p-methyl phenyl)sulfide and found that it had a high energy level alignment with the perovskite and a high electron mobility, making it a promising candidate as an HTM for PSCs. Overall, a comprehensive evaluation and comparison with other commonly used HTMs using both experimental and theoretical results would provide a more comprehensive understanding of the potential advantages and limitations of the proposed doped h-BANDs and their suitability as HTMs for PSCs.

The FF and the VOC of the HBNQDs and EFHBNQDs are provided in Table 5. As shown in Table 5, the highest VOC values of 0.93, 1.10, and 1.15 eV, respectively, were related to HBNQD-CH3, HBNQD-OCH3, and HBNQD-SCH3. Table 5 summarizes the results related to the parameters for HTMs for h-BNQD and h-BNQD-Ys, comparing them with those of STAD. As shown in Table 5, EFHBNQD-SCH3 was the only suitable HTM since it met the requirements. Furthermore, the EFHBNQDs exhibited greater HM and OCV in comparison with STAD. So, it is possible to use EFHBNQD-SCH3 as promising HTMs in PVSCs.

The possibility of employing HBNQDs and EFHBNQDs as hole transport materials (HTMs) in perovskite photovoltaic cells (PVSCs) was investigated through density functional theory (DFT) and time-dependent DFT (TDDFT) calculations, and it was concluded that only HBNQD-SCH3 was suitable as an HTM due to its favorable properties, such as high electron mobility, good solubility, and sufficient photostability. The main purpose of employing HTMs in PVSCs is to transport charges from the perovskite to an electrode, where the energy levels of the HTM must be aligned with the perovskite and the electrode. The chosen perovskite had typical energy levels of −3.93 eV for its valence band and −5.43 eV for its conducting band. For HBNQDs and EFHBNQDs to be suitable as HTMs, their HOMO level must be greater than the valence band of the perovskite, their HOMO level must be lower than or equal to the energy level of the gold electrode, and their LUMO level must be greater than the conducting band of the perovskite. The DFT computations revealed that of the three compounds, HBNQD-CH3, HBNQD-CH3, and HBNQD-SCH3, only HBNQD-SCH3 had suitable energy levels for use as an HTM. Furthermore, the solubility of HBNQDs and EFHBNQDs in different organic solvents was investigated, with HBNQD-SCH3 shown to be the most soluble. UV–vis spectroscopy was used to analyze the optical properties of the compounds, with suitable compounds having high light-harvesting efficiency and electron separation. Furthermore, the compounds might have higher maximum hole mobility (HM) compared to other benzene derivatives used as HTMs in PVSCs. Based on these analyses, HBNQD-SCH3 was considered to be the most suitable HTM of the compounds investigated.
